provenance.source_corpus: Effects of reforestation on near‐surface saturated hydraulic conductivity in a managed forest landscape, southern Ontario, Canada

source_spans.text: Reforestation of previously deforested landscapes often increases soil organic matter content and porosity as the plantation forest ages, leading to increased soil saturated hydraulic conductivity (KH).
leading to increased soil saturated hydraulic conductivity (KH).
Temporal trends in KH were associated with increased understory density with forest age
suggesting that increases in KH were driven by understory root development and greater macroporosity.
suggesting that increases in KH were driven by understory root development and greater macroporosity.
This recovery may be assisted by management activities such as thinning of red pine stands, which promotes understory development.
Our results indicate that reforestation of a previously degraded landscape in southern Ontario has resulted in the hydrologic recovery of the soil's saturated hydraulic conductivity.
